metric_value: Less than 10% of RS/6000 AIX 3.2.5 sites migrated to AIX 4 within 18 months of launch
notes: Aberdeen field data; high-confidence primary finding

metric_value: Approximately 90% of RS/6000 users remained on AIX 3.2.5 as of April 1996
notes: Derived from <10% migration figure

metric_value: IBM froze AIX 3.2.5 in-time in 1995; no new features, only PTFs
notes: IBM decision to freeze version 3

metric_value: Biweekly PTF stream was primary pain point preventing upgrades; frozen in 1995
notes: User frustration cited as #1 barrier to AIX 4 adoption

metric_value: AIX 4 is future-driven by SMP; RS/6000 user base did not initially have SMP hardware
notes: SMP labeled 'ho-hum' at launch due to lack of SMP hardware in base

metric_value: AIX 4's VRMF architecture enables modular upgrades; production-grade OS for future
notes: Aberdeen characterizes VRMF as key architectural advance over PTF model

metric_value: HACMP clustering now thrives with AIX 4; significant improvement over AIX 3 compatibility
notes: AIX 4 enabling clustering as a production workload

metric_value: Production-grade, flexible Unix OS for enterprise line-of-business applications
notes: Aberdeen classification of AIX 4 strategic role

metric_value: Small business users blocked by VAR applications not yet migrated to AIX 4; ISVs also suffered version-3 fatigue
notes: VAR dependency cited as 4th most common reason for non-migration

metric_value: Coupling OS upgrade with PowerPC hardware upgrade classified as high-risk production practice
notes: Aberdeen strongly cautions against simultaneous OS+HW upgrades

metric_value: Majority of users feared AIX 4 would repeat AIX 3 patch frustration
notes: Factor 1 of Aberdeen's multi-factor barrier analysis

metric_value: Large portions planning simultaneous Power2→PowerPC hardware + OS upgrade
notes: Factor 2 of barrier analysis

metric_name: Reason for non-migration: frozen environment policy
metric_value: Many AIX 3 managers froze production environment under 'if it ain't broke' philosophy
notes: Factor 3 of barrier analysis

metric_value: Some niche suppliers abandoned AIX and left customers locked into version 3
notes: Factor 4 of barrier analysis

metric_name: AIX 4 mass adoption by end of 1996
metric_value: Aberdeen believes AIX users still on 3.2.5 by end of 1996 will be doing a disservice to their enterprises
notes: Prediction that AIX 4 adoption will accelerate; pairs with OBS-016

metric_value: AIX 4 became the dominant AIX version through the late 1990s; IBM released AIX 5L in 2001 evolving the platform further
notes: Prediction confirmed; AIX 3.2.5 fully supplanted; AIX line continues through version 7.3 in 2026

metric_value: Aberdeen predicts SMP will become the primary driver of AIX 4 adoption as RS/6000 SMP hardware ships
notes: Pairs with OBS-018

metric_value: IBM RS/6000 SMP models became mainstream by 1997-98; AIX 4's SMP support proved essential for enterprise adoption
notes: SMP prediction confirmed; RS/6000 SMP systems became standard enterprise configurations

metric_value: Aberdeen: reward-to-risk ratio so great that RS/6000 customers should move to AIX 4 as fast as they can in 1996
notes: Strongly worded investment thesis from Aberdeen

metric_value: IBM cannot force VARs who suffered version-3 frustration to upgrade until they are very confident in AIX 4 stability
notes: Structural challenge in ecosystem upgrade dynamics
